channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.eurogamer.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.eurogamer.net

item.title: CD Projekt Red reveals huge The Witcher 3 sales milestone, which helps explain why a third expansion is in development 11 years later
item.content:encoded:
"The return of Geralt after more than one decade will be an excellent adventure."

Why is CD Projekt Red making a third expansion for The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt, more than a decade after the original game was released? A newly revealed sales milestone of 65 million units probably has something to do with it. That's a hell of an installed base to sell to.

CD Projekt Red announced The Witcher 3 sales milestone in a quarterly financial update this afternoon. It did so while referencing yesterday's Songs of the Past expansion news and suggesting the new adventure might be many people's first encounter with The Witcher universe, which is an interesting take.

Watch on YouTube

"This third full-fledged expansion for The Witcher 3, previously mentioned as one of our unannounced projects, is currently at an advanced stage of development," CD Projekt Red joint CEO Michal Nowakowski said. "The return of Geralt after more than one decade will be an excellent adventure, and a trip down memory lane, before we pass the baton to Ciri in the upcoming The Witcher 4.

"As for The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt," Nowakowski added, "it reached another notable sales milestone, with more than 65m copies of the game now sold. This translates into a huge community of gamers for whom the new expansion provides an excellent opportunity to again hit the trail with the White Wolf. We also see great potential for Songs of the Past to become the first encounter with The Witcher universe, as well as the beginning of a fresh adventure, for many gamers, even as we await the release of The Witcher 4."

The remarkable enduring success of The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt has plenty to do with its five-star quality, but also with CD Projekt Red's tireless support of the game. It has released an endless stream of updates to bring things like mod support long after release, and support for advanced, modern graphical techniques. After all, much has changed in 11 years, which is reflected in CD Projekt Red's decision to significantly change the minimum PC requirements for The Witcher 3 for the new expansion's launch next year.

The mission to spread The Witcher 3 far and wide ahead of the expansion saw the Complete Edition of the game released on Xbox Game Pass Premium earlier this year, too,

Actual details on the new Witcher 3 expansion Songs of the Past are thin, though we know it'll star Geralt rather than Ciri, who's leading The Witcher 4. When in The Witcher 3 timeline it takes place, though, we don't know. There's lots of speculation surrounding the sword Geralt is seen unsheathing in the expansion artwork as well. More details will be revealed at the end of the summer.

Mentioned in this article



https://www.eurogamer.net/witcher-3-sales-milestone-65-million
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Unlike The Sims, early access hit Paralives will "never" have paid DLC and will stick to free updates, devs say
item.content:encoded:
"We are happy to deliver that."

Though we'd known about it for quite some time, breezy life simulator Paralives has managed to become one of this month's surprise hits. With over 250k copies sold in less than eight hours, it was off to a great start on Steam earlier this week, and now the developers are promising no paid DLC will be released.

That's exactly what lead developer Alex Massé of Paralives Studio said during a Reddit AMA: "There will never be paid DLCs, only free updates! Even after the Early Access. As life sim fans, we wanted to make a game we would like to play ourselves without the need to purchase a lot of extra content and we are happy to deliver that." Comparisons to EA's The Sims series are obvious, and this stance - now backed by the game's success - feels like a direct answer to life sim fans' demands over the years.

"Because the team is small (only 15 people), the sales we made so far will sustain us for many years, even if we increase the size of the team so we're safe and really grateful for that," they added. It's sound logic which signals Alex Massé and his team are looking to stay sustainable versus growing too much too fast.

While the game's current total sales number is unknown, 250k units sold in less than half a day is a mighty feat for a team that's been flying under many radars, despite Paralives earning notable support in the road to early access. The game even hit a respectable 78,603 peak of concurrent players three days ago. The studio's stance will also no doubt be popular given the notoriety of its rivals - namely The Sims - when it comes to charging a small fortune for similarly small additions.

Eurogamer's Matt Wales has been enjoying his time with Paralives, noting it's "an incredibly promising start" in its current shape that "makes for an Instagram-ready rendition of life that feels about as far removed from the chaos of The Sims as you can get".

Mentioned in this article



https://www.eurogamer.net/paralives-devs-say-no-paid-dlc-plans
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
